title: GraphTypes
lean_code_with_comments:
/-- The CDMG `G` is a *Conditional Acyclic Directed Mixed Graph
(CADMG)*: it is acyclic. No restriction on inputs `J` or bidirected
edges `L`. -/
def IsCADMG (G : CDMG α) : Prop := G.IsAcyclic

/-- The CDMG `G` is a *Directed Mixed Graph* (DMG): it has no input
nodes. Acyclicity is not assumed; bidirected edges `L` are
allowed. -/
def IsDMG (G : CDMG α) : Prop := G.J = ∅

/-- The CDMG `G` is an *Acyclic Directed Mixed Graph* (ADMG): it is
acyclic and has no input nodes. Bidirected edges `L` are
allowed. -/
def IsADMG (G : CDMG α) : Prop := G.IsAcyclic ∧ G.J = ∅

/-- The CDMG `G` is a *Conditional Directed Graph* (CDG): it has no
bidirected edges. Inputs `J` are allowed; acyclicity is not
assumed. -/
def IsCDG (G : CDMG α) : Prop := G.L = ∅

/-- The CDMG `G` is a *Directed Graph* (DG): no input nodes and no
bidirected edges. Acyclicity is not assumed; directed cycles are
allowed. -/
def IsDG (G : CDMG α) : Prop := G.J = ∅ ∧ G.L = ∅

/-- The CDMG `G` is a *Conditional Directed Acyclic Graph* (CDAG):
it is acyclic and has no bidirected edges. Inputs `J` are
allowed. -/
def IsCDAG (G : CDMG α) : Prop := G.IsAcyclic ∧ G.L = ∅

/-- The CDMG `G` is a *Directed Acyclic Graph* (DAG): it is acyclic,
has no input nodes and no bidirected edges. -/
def IsDAG (G : CDMG α) : Prop := G.IsAcyclic ∧ G.J = ∅ ∧ G.L = ∅
lean_code_without_comments:
def IsCADMG (G : CDMG α) : Prop := G.IsAcyclic

def IsDMG (G : CDMG α) : Prop := G.J = ∅

def IsADMG (G : CDMG α) : Prop := G.IsAcyclic ∧ G.J = ∅

def IsCDG (G : CDMG α) : Prop := G.L = ∅

def IsDG (G : CDMG α) : Prop := G.J = ∅ ∧ G.L = ∅

def IsCDAG (G : CDMG α) : Prop := G.IsAcyclic ∧ G.L = ∅

def IsDAG (G : CDMG α) : Prop := G.IsAcyclic ∧ G.J = ∅ ∧ G.L = ∅
lean_explanation:
Definition 3.7 is a glossary: starting from a Conditional Directed Mixed Graph $G = (J, V, E, L)$, the LN attaches seven acronyms — CADMG, DMG, ADMG, CDG, DG, CDAG, DAG — to the combinations of at most three side conditions used as preconditions by the rest of the notes. Each name is encoded in Lean as a separate `Prop`-valued predicate inside the `CDMG` namespace, so the LN's recurring prose "$G$ is a CADMG", "$G$ is a DAG" lifts verbatim into Lean as `G.IsCADMG`, `G.IsDAG`, and so on.

The ambient binder `α : Type*` is the vertex carrier (the LN's silent assumption that the graph lives over some type), and `G : CDMG α` is the bundled record from def 3.1. The three atomic ingredients combined by the seven names are: `G.IsAcyclic` — the predicate of def 3.6 asserting that no node admits a non-trivial directed walk back to itself, the Lean counterpart of the LN's "$G$ is acyclic"; `G.J = ∅` — the LN's $J = \emptyset$, where `J` is the input-node field of the CDMG record; and `G.L = ∅` — the LN's $L = \emptyset$, the analogous condition on the bidirected-edge field.

Each of the seven definitions is then a one-line `def` whose body is the `∧`-conjunction of whichever subset of those three the LN lists for that name: `IsCADMG := IsAcyclic`; `IsDMG := J = ∅`; `IsADMG := IsAcyclic ∧ J = ∅`; `IsCDG := L = ∅`; `IsDG := J = ∅ ∧ L = ∅`; `IsCDAG := IsAcyclic ∧ L = ∅`; `IsDAG := IsAcyclic ∧ J = ∅ ∧ L = ∅`. The conjuncts are spelled out inline rather than chained through smaller names. No exotic Mathlib idioms appear — just `Prop`, `∧`, and `=` on `Set`s; the only naming convention worth flagging is the `Is` prefix, which follows both the in-file `IsAcyclic` precedent and Mathlib's pervasive `IsAntisymm` / `IsTrans` / `IsAcyclic` style for `Prop`-valued predicates. The eighth combination — no side conditions — is a plain CDMG and gets no separate name in the LN, so no eighth predicate appears here.
design_choices:
**`Prop`-valued predicates, not subtypes or new structures.** Throughout chapters 4–16 the LN consistently writes "$G$ is acyclic" or "$G$ is a CADMG" as a hypothesis on a theorem, never as part of the ambient graph type. A subtype `{G : CDMG α // G.IsAcyclic}`, or a parallel `structure CADMG` bundling acyclicity in as a field, would force every later signature through a coercion `↑G : CDMG α`, break compatibility with the existing `G.IsAcyclic` predicate from def 3.6, and treat CADMGs as mathematically distinct from CDMGs — which the LN explicitly does not. Predicates are the lightest encoding that preserves the LN's "$G$ is a [acronym]" framing verbatim at every later use site.

**Predicates live in the `Causality.CDMG` namespace and carry an `Is` prefix.** Placing each name inside the `CDMG` namespace turns it into a dot-projection: `G.IsCADMG`, `G.IsDAG`, …, reading aloud as "$G$ is a CADMG" / "$G$ is a DAG" and matching the LN's prose directly. The `Is` prefix matches the in-file `IsAcyclic` precedent (def 3.6) and Mathlib's pervasive `IsAntisymm` / `IsTrans` / `IsAcyclic` convention; saying `G.IsDAG` aloud is mildly awkward, but a uniform prefix flagging "this is a property of `G`" pays for itself at every downstream use site.

**Inline conjunctions, not nested aliases.** Each predicate spells out its full content (e.g. `IsDAG := IsAcyclic ∧ J = ∅ ∧ L = ∅`) rather than building on a smaller name (e.g. `IsDAG := IsADMG ∧ L = ∅`). Two reasons. First, unfolding hygiene: a nested alias would force a caller proving `G.IsDAG` from its three atomic ingredients to unfold two layers and re-associate the conjunction every time. Second, there is no canonical nesting — is a DAG an ADMG without bidirected edges, or a CDAG without inputs, or both? The LN sidesteps the question by listing each name's full content, and we follow suit.

**All seven names are kept, even though some imply others.** Logically `IsDAG → IsADMG → IsDMG` and `IsDAG → IsCDAG → IsCDG`, so a minimal axiomatisation would only need the three atomic predicates plus `And`. We keep all seven because every one is the name a downstream chapter uses for its preconditions — chapter 5 do-calculus assumes a CADMG, chapter 16 FCI assumes an ADMG, chapter 4 plain CBNs assume a DAG, and so on. Collapsing the names into atomics would force every later theorem statement to spell out the three conjuncts instead of reading "$G$ is a [name]".

**`def` rather than `abbrev`.** An `abbrev` would unfold to its right-hand side automatically at every use site; downstream callers would then see goals like `G.IsAcyclic ∧ G.J = ∅` instead of `G.IsADMG`, losing the readable name. The `def` keeps the LN's name on the page; callers who genuinely need the underlying conjunction can `unfold CDMG.IsADMG` (a one-liner) or destructure the hypothesis via `obtain ⟨_, _⟩ := hG`.
